title: Dog Bleeding from Penis: Triage Guide & Next Steps
meta_desc: Learn why your dog may have blood dripping from his penis, the urgency of the situation, and how to help your vet with a quick diagnosis.
content_html:
What Does Bleeding from the Penis Mean?

Finding blood dripping from your dog’s penis—whether it is mixed with urine or dripping independently—is a sign that something is wrong within the urogenital system. Common causes include urinary tract infections (UTIs), bladder stones, or issues with the prostate gland (especially in unneutered males). It can also be caused by trauma to the area, or more rarely, tumors or blood clotting disorders.

Is This an Emergency? (Urgency: Medium)

In most cases, constant dripping of blood from the penis is considered a Medium Urgency situation. If your dog is acting normally, eating, and successfully passing urine, you should schedule a veterinary appointment for the same day or the next morning. However, it becomes a High Urgency emergency if your dog is straining to urinate but producing nothing, as this could indicate a life-threatening blockage.

Why Taking a Photo Helps Triage

Capturing a clear photo or video of the dripping can be an invaluable tool for your veterinary team. A visual record helps the vet determine the exact color, volume, and frequency of the bleeding, which can be difficult to describe accurately in person. It also helps them see if the blood is bright red (fresh) or darker (older), aiding in a faster and more efficient diagnosis during your visit.

faqs.question: Could my dog's prostate be the cause?
faqs.answer: Yes, particularly in intact male dogs. Conditions like benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) or prostate infections are common causes of penile bleeding.

faqs.question: Should I prevent my dog from licking the area?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is highly recommended to use an Elizabethan collar (cone) to prevent your dog from licking, as excessive licking can worsen inflammation and lead to secondary infections.

faqs.question: What information should I have ready for the vet?
faqs.answer: Be ready to tell the vet if your dog is neutered, how long the bleeding has been happening, if he is straining to urinate, and if his behavior or appetite has changed.
